Mobius inversion with a two-sided valuation twist feeding an LTE plus CRT multiplicative-order computation

Let $p=3$ and $q=11$ be primes, let $k=4$, and set $n_0=p^k q^2=3^4\cdot 11^2=9801$. Fix $A=9$ and $B=1$.
For each divisor $n\mid n_0$, write $n=p^i q^j$ where $i=v_p(n)$ and $j=v_q(n)$.
Define $F$ on divisors of $n_0$ by the following rules:
If $j=0$ and $i=0$, set $F(1)=1$.
If $j=0$ and $i\ge 1$, set $F(p^i)=p^i+Bp^{i-1}$.
If $j\ge 1$ and $i=0$, set $F(q^j)=q^j+Aq^{j-1}$.
If $j\ge 1$ and $i\ge 1$, set $F(p^i q^j)=p^i q^j + A p^i q^{j-1} + B p^{i-1} q^j$.
Assume an arithmetic function $f$ on the divisors of $n_0$ satisfies $F(n)=\sum_{d\mid n} f(d)$ for every $n\mid n_0$.
Define $t=v_p(f(n_0))$ and $M=f(n_0)/p^t$.
Determine the least positive integer $r$ such that $M^r\equiv 1(\mathrm{mod}\ n_0)$.

Solution.
Step 1 (Dirichlet_convolution_optional).
On the set of positive divisors of $n_0$, define Dirichlet convolution by $(g*h)(n)=\sum_{d\mid n} g(d)h(n/d)$, and define $\mathbf{1}(n)=1$ for all $n\mid n_0$.
Then $\sum_{d\mid n} f(d)=(f*\mathbf{1})(n)$, so the hypothesis is $F=f*\mathbf{1}$ on divisors of $n_0$.

Step 2 (mobius_inversion_optional).
Let $\mu$ be the Mobius function restricted to divisors of $n_0$, and let $\varepsilon$ be the identity for Dirichlet convolution on this divisor lattice, so $\varepsilon(1)=1$ and $\varepsilon(n)=0$ for $n>1$.
Mobius inversion is the statement $\mu*\mathbf{1}=\varepsilon$.
Convolving $F=f*\mathbf{1}$ with $\mu$ gives $F*\mu=f*(\mathbf{1}*\mu)=f*\varepsilon=f$.
In particular,
$f(n_0)=\sum_{d\mid n_0} \mu(d)\,F(n_0/d)$.

Step 3 (mu_mobius_optional) - Lemma 1 (squarefree collapse for $n_0=p^k q^2$).
For $n_0=p^k q^2$, the only squarefree divisors are $1$, $p$, $q$, $pq$. Hence
$f(n_0)=F(n_0)-F(n_0/p)-F(n_0/q)+F(n_0/(pq))$.

Proof (Lemma 1).
In Step 2, terms with $\mu(d)=0$ vanish, and $\mu(d)=0$ exactly when $d$ is divisible by a square.
Every divisor of $n_0=p^k q^2$ has the form $p^a q^b$ with $0\le a\le k$ and $0\le b\le 2$, so the squarefree ones are exactly those with $a,b\in\{0,1\}$, namely $1,p,q,pq$.
Also $\mu(1)=1$, $\mu(p)=\mu(q)=-1$, $\mu(pq)=1$.
Substituting into Step 2 gives the four-term formula.

Step 4 (multiplicative_functions and $v_p,v_q$ bookkeeping).
Compute the four values in Lemma 1 using $p=3$, $q=11$, $k=4$, $A=9$, $B=1$.
All four inputs $n_0$, $n_0/p$, $n_0/q$, $n_0/(pq)$ have $i\ge 1$ and $j\ge 1$, so we use
$F(p^i q^j)=p^i q^j + A p^i q^{j-1} + B p^{i-1} q^j$.

1) For $n_0=3^4\cdot 11^2$ we have $(i,j)=(4,2)$:
$F(n_0)=3^4\cdot 11^2 + 9\cdot 3^4\cdot 11 + 1\cdot 3^3\cdot 11^2
=9801+8019+3267
=21087$.

2) For $n_0/p=3^3\cdot 11^2$ we have $(i,j)=(3,2)$:
$F(n_0/p)=3^3\cdot 11^2 + 9\cdot 3^3\cdot 11 + 1\cdot 3^2\cdot 11^2
=3267+2673+1089
=7029$.

3) For $n_0/q=3^4\cdot 11$ we have $(i,j)=(4,1)$:
$F(n_0/q)=3^4\cdot 11 + 9\cdot 3^4 + 1\cdot 3^3\cdot 11
=891+729+297
=1917$.

4) For $n_0/(pq)=3^3\cdot 11$ we have $(i,j)=(3,1)$:
$F(n_0/(pq))=3^3\cdot 11 + 9\cdot 3^3 + 1\cdot 3^2\cdot 11
=297+243+99
=639$.

Step 5 (mobius_inversion_optional applied).
By Lemma 1 and Step 4,
$f(n_0)=21087-7029-1917+639$.
Compute $21087-7029=14058$, then $14058-1917=12141$, then $12141+639=12780$.
So $f(n_0)=12780$.

Step 6 (v_p_valuation).
Here $p=3$. Compute $t=v_3(12780)$:
$12780/3=4260$, $4260/3=1420$, and $3\nmid 1420$.
So $t=2$ and $M=f(n_0)/3^t=12780/9=1420$.

Step 7 (unit check and local data).
We verify $\mathrm{gcd}(M,n_0)=1$.
Since $1420\equiv 1(\mathrm{mod}\ 3)$ and $1420\equiv 1(\mathrm{mod}\ 11)$, we have $\mathrm{gcd}(1420,3\cdot 11)=1$, hence $\mathrm{gcd}(1420,3^4\cdot 11^2)=1$, so $\mathrm{gcd}(M,n_0)=1$.
Also $M-1=1419=3\cdot 11\cdot 43$, so $v_3(M-1)=1$ and $v_{11}(M-1)=1$.

Step 8 (lifting_the_exponent_LTE) - Lemma 2 (order from $v_\ell(a-1)$).
Let $\ell$ be an odd prime, let $a$ be an integer with $\ell\nmid a$, and let $s=v_\ell(a-1)\ge 1$.
Then for every integer $m\ge 0$,
$v_\ell(a^{\ell^m}-1)=s+m$.
Consequently, for every integer $e>s$, the multiplicative order of $a$ modulo $\ell^e$ equals $\ell^{e-s}$.

Proof (Lemma 2).
Apply LTE to $a^{\ell^m}-1=a^{\ell^m}-1^{\ell^m}$.
Since $\ell$ is odd, $\ell\mid (a-1)$, and $\ell\nmid a\cdot 1$, LTE gives
$v_\ell(a^{\ell^m}-1)=v_\ell(a-1)+v_\ell(\ell^m)=s+m$.

Now fix $e>s$ and set $m_0=e-s$.
Then $v_\ell(a^{\ell^{m_0}}-1)=s+m_0=e$, so $a^{\ell^{m_0}}\equiv 1(\mathrm{mod}\ \ell^e)$.
Hence the order $r=\mathrm{ord}_{\ell^e}(a)$ divides $\ell^{m_0}=\ell^{e-s}$, so $r$ is a power of $\ell$.

If $m_0\ge 1$, then $v_\ell(a^{\ell^{m_0-1}}-1)=s+m_0-1=e-1<e$, so $a^{\ell^{m_0-1}}\not\equiv 1(\mathrm{mod}\ \ell^e)$.
Because $r$ is a power of $\ell$ dividing $\ell^{m_0}$, this forces $r=\ell^{m_0}=\ell^{e-s}$.

Step 9 (multiplicative_order, prime-power stages).
Apply Lemma 2 to $a=M=1420$.

For $\ell=3$, we have $s=v_3(M-1)=1$ and $e=4$, so $\mathrm{ord}_{3^4}(M)=3^{4-1}=27$.

For $\ell=11$, we have $s=v_{11}(M-1)=1$ and $e=2$, so $\mathrm{ord}_{11^2}(M)=11^{2-1}=11$.

Step 10 (Chinese_remainder_theorem and order-lcm) - Lemma 3.
If $\mathrm{gcd}(m,n)=1$ and $\mathrm{gcd}(a,mn)=1$, then $\mathrm{ord}_{mn}(a)=\mathrm{lcm}(\mathrm{ord}_m(a),\mathrm{ord}_n(a))$.

Proof (Lemma 3).
Let $r_1=\mathrm{ord}_m(a)$ and $r_2=\mathrm{ord}_n(a)$.
Then $a^{r_1}\equiv 1(\mathrm{mod}\ m)$ and $a^{r_2}\equiv 1(\mathrm{mod}\ n)$, so $a^{\mathrm{lcm}(r_1,r_2)}\equiv 1$ modulo both $m$ and $n$.
Since $\mathrm{gcd}(m,n)=1$, CRT implies $a^{\mathrm{lcm}(r_1,r_2)}\equiv 1(\mathrm{mod}\ mn)$.
Thus $\mathrm{ord}_{mn}(a)$ divides $\mathrm{lcm}(r_1,r_2)$.

Conversely, if $a^r\equiv 1(\mathrm{mod}\ mn)$ then $a^r\equiv 1(\mathrm{mod}\ m)$ and $a^r\equiv 1(\mathrm{mod}\ n)$, so $r$ is a multiple of both $r_1$ and $r_2$, hence a multiple of $\mathrm{lcm}(r_1,r_2)$.
Therefore the least such $r$ equals $\mathrm{lcm}(r_1,r_2)$.

Step 11 (final computation).
We have $n_0=3^4\cdot 11^2$ with $\mathrm{gcd}(3^4,11^2)=1$.
By Lemma 3 and Step 9,
$\mathrm{ord}_{n_0}(M)=\mathrm{lcm}(27,11)=297$.
Thus the least positive $r$ with $M^r\equiv 1(\mathrm{mod}\ n_0)$ is $297$.

Answer: $297$.

Uniqueness Test
1) Uniqueness of $f(n_0)$, hence of $t$ and $M$.
Step 1 rewrites the hypothesis as $F=f*\mathbf{1}$ on the divisor lattice of $n_0$.
Mobius inversion in Step 2 forces the unique solution $f=F*\mu$.
Hence $f(n_0)$ is uniquely determined by $F$, so $t=v_p(f(n_0))$ and $M=f(n_0)/p^t$ are uniquely determined integers.

2) Uniqueness of $r$.
Since $\mathrm{gcd}(M,n_0)=1$ (Step 7), the congruence $M^r\equiv 1(\mathrm{mod}\ n_0)$ holds for at least one positive $r$, for example $r=\varphi(n_0)$ by Euler's theorem.
The set of positive integers $r$ with $M^r\equiv 1(\mathrm{mod}\ n_0)$ therefore has a least element, and that least element is unique.
This least element is by definition the multiplicative order of $M$ modulo $n_0$.

3) How the instance forces the value $297$.
Step 7 fixes $v_3(M-1)=1$ and $v_{11}(M-1)=1$.
Lemma 2 then forces $\mathrm{ord}_{3^4}(M)=27$ and $\mathrm{ord}_{11^2}(M)=11$.
Lemma 3 forces the global order to be $\mathrm{lcm}(27,11)=297$.
No other positive integer can be the least such exponent.

297
